author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
Whether Faith Be in a Man’s Own Power.
title: Whether Faith Be in a Man’s Own Power.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Attend now to the point which we have laid down for discussion: whether faith is in our own power?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: We now speak of that faith which we employ when we believe anything, not that which we give when we make a promise; for this too is called faith.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: We use the word in one sense when we say, “He had no faith in me,” and in another sense when we say, “He did not keep faith with me.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: The one phrase means, “He did not believe what I said;” the other, “He did not do what he promised.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: According to the faith by which we believe, we are faithful to God; but according to that whereby a thing is brought to pass which is promised, God Himself even is faithful to us; for the apostle declares, “God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Well, now, the former is the faith about which we inquire, Whether it be in our power? even the faith by which we believe God, or believe on God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For of this it is written, “Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: And again, “To him that believeth on Him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Consider now whether anybody believes, if he be unwilling; or whether he believes not, if he shall have willed it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Such a position, indeed, is absurd (for what is believing but consenting to the truth of what is said? and this consent is certainly voluntary): faith, therefore, is in our own power.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But, as the apostle says: “There is no power but comes from God,” what reason then is there why it may not be said to us even of this: “What hast thou which thou hast not received?”—for it is God who gave us even to believe.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Nowhere, however, in Holy Scripture do we find such an assertion as, There is no volition but comes from God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: And rightly is it not so written, because it is not true: otherwise God would be the author even of sins (which Heaven forbid!), if there were no volition except what comes from Him; inasmuch as an evil volition alone is already a sin, even if the effect be wanting,—in other words, if it has not ability.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But when the evil volition receives ability to accomplish its intention, this proceeds from the judgment of God, with whom there is no unrighteousness.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: He indeed punishes after this manner; nor is His chastisement unjust because it is secret.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: The ungodly man, however, is not aware that he is being punished, except when he unwillingly discovers by an open penalty how much evil he has willingly committed.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: This is just what the apostle says of certain men: “God hath given them up to the evil desires of their own hearts, . . .to do those things that are not convenient.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Accordingly, the Lord also said to Pilate: “Thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee from above.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But still, when the ability is given, surely no necessity is imposed.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Therefore, although David had received ability to kill Saul, he preferred sparing to striking him.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Whence we understand that bad men receive ability for the condemnation of their depraved will, while good men receive ability for trying of their good will.
